channel.title: Latest alerts from washingtonpost.com
channel.description: An unofficial feed created by Derek Willis.

item.title: Portland figured out a wonky secret to get cheaper houses built. Other cities are copying.
item.description: The Oregon city legalized six-plexes, tiny houses and backyard homes, and then devised a way to actually get them built.

item.title: Fed holds rates steady as war in Iran clouds outlook
item.description: The Persian Gulf conflict threatens to reignite inflation while slowing growth, complicating the central bank’s work.

item.title: Oil prices continue upward climb as White House effort to ease shipping restrictions has little impact
item.description: President Donald Trump’s suspension of the Jones Act to free up shipping did little to calm markets. The average cost of unleaded gas is now 32 percent higher than a month ago.

item.title: Iranian regime is degraded but still intact, Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard says in Senate testimony
item.description: Gabbard provided the Senate Intelligence Committee with mixed messages about the state of Iran’s nuclear program before the war began.

item.title: DHS pick Mullin says immigration agents will use judicial warrants and retreats from comments about Alex Pretti, Renée Good
item.description: Sen. Markwayne Mullin (R-Oklahoma), President Donald Trump’s pick to head the Department of Homeland Security, is appearing before the Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs for a confirmation hearing.

item.title: Americans brought a picnic to the Civil War. This is even worse.
item.description: The Iran war isn’t entertainment. The White House should stop treating it as such.

item.title: The steak we ordered was rotten, but the restaurant said it was fine: Our food critic is taking questions
item.description: Post food critic Elazar Sontag entertains your restaurant questions, rants and raves.

item.title: This island is the topic of today’s On the Record
item.description: Answer now to earn your Wednesday points.

item.title: This Emirati billionaire put a voice to Gulf anger over Trump’s war in Iran
item.description: A hotel magnate lambasted the president on social media for not considering collateral damage, although he later told The Post: “I blame Trump, but I blame the Iranians more.”

item.title: Iran war. Resignation letter. Illinois primaries. Voice of America. Housing costs. Heat wave. Boomer iPad use. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: Analysis: Is MAGA in its cringe era?
item.description: To his enthusiastic young loyalists, Donald Trump's victory in 2024 marked a cultural realignment that rejected the liberal agenda in favor of something ostensibly cooler. But the vibes have shifted.

item.title: Judge reinstates 1,000 Voice of America employees, deems firings illegal
item.description: U.S. District Judge Royce C. Lamberth ruled that the near-total shutdown of the U.S. Agency for Global Media, which oversees VOA and funds several international broadcasters such as Radio Free Asia, violated federal administrative law. He ordered the full-time employees to return to work by March 23, and told the agency to resume international broadcasting.

item.title: Democrats debate how far left to swing in fight for key Senate seat in Michigan
item.description: Abdul El-Sayed, backed by Sen. Bernie Sanders, is betting a populist economic agenda is Democrats’ path back to power in a state that has traditionally produced more moderate senators.

item.title: House panel subpoenas Attorney General Pam Bondi in Epstein investigation
item.description: The House Oversight Committee ordered the attorney general to appear on April 14. The Justice Department says the subpoena is “unnecessary.”

item.title: U.S. tells all its embassies to ‘immediately’ review security after strikes
item.description: The State Department order follows persistent attacks on U.S. missions, with almost 300 reported in Iraq alone, according to an internal department count.

item.title: This is where Will Leitch is looking for joy and diversion
item.description: Every Thursday, his newsletter will unpack the stories and moments we can’t stop thinking about.

item.title: Israel urges Iranians to revolt but privately assesses they’ll be ‘slaughtered,’ cable says
item.description: Israeli officials told their U.S. counterparts they hope for an uprising even though it would lead to a massacre, according to a State Department cable reviewed by The Post.

item.title: Top Gabbard counterterrorism official Joe Kent resigns in opposition to Iran war
item.description: “After much reflection, I have decided to resign,” Joe Kent, the head of the National Counterterrorism Center and close aide to Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard, posted on X. “I cannot in good conscience support the ongoing war in Iran.’’

item.title: What does it cost to die in America? Search your Zip code.
item.description: Calculate how much you should set aside for the final years of life.

item.title: An Oscars speech in today’s On the Record
item.description: Answer now to earn your Tuesday points.

item.title: Iran’s regime. Cuba threat. Trump ballroom. Kids’ vaccines. Climate deaths. Strength training. Shark friends. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: Israel says it killed Ali Larijani, Iran’s top security official, in strike
item.description: Israel’s defense minister said Larijani, head of the Supreme National Security Council, and Basij commander Gholamreza Soleimani were “eliminated” in airstrikes.

item.title: Cuba’s national energy grid collapses, causing island-wide blackout amid Trump administration’s oil blockade
item.description: President Donald Trump threatened again to topple the country’s communist government. “I do believe I’ll have the honor of taking Cuba,” he told reporters Monday. Asked whether this meant diplomacy or military action, he said: “I think I can do anything I want with it.”

item.title: Federal judge blocks RFK Jr.'s vaccine policy overhaul for now
item.description: A federal judge on Monday blocked the Trump administration from implementing sweeping changes to the nation’s childhood immunization schedule, mostly siding with major medical organizations that argue Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. unlawfully altered vaccine policy and improperly reconstituted a federal vaccine advisory panel.

item.title: Board chosen by Trump approves his plan to close Kennedy Center for two years
item.description: The John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts will move forward with President Donald Trump’s plans to shutter the institution for two years after its board of trustees voted Monday to approve the proposal — clearing the way for sweeping renovations at the nation’s premier performing arts center.

item.title: Teens file lawsuit alleging Elon Musk’s Grok chatbot made sexual images of them as minors
item.description: Three plaintiffs, two of whom are minors, accuse xAI of distributing, possessing and producing child pornography made by editing clothed photos taken from social media and other sources.

item.title: Susie Wiles, White House chief of staff, has breast cancer, Trump says
item.description: The president’s longtime confidante plans to continue working while receiving treatment.

item.title: Carolyn Hax: Expected to show up for family who never show up for you
item.description: Since childhood, letter writer was told to show up for older siblings who never reciprocated. Is there some kind of rule?

item.title: A rare threat of tornadoes, thunderstorms and damaging winds covers a swath of the East Coast. Here's what to plan for.
item.description: A Level 4 out of 5 risk — typically only occurring two or three times per decade in the region — extends from Western Maryland to South Carolina and follows the Interstate 95 corridor from D.C. to Richmond to near Raleigh, North Carolina. That risk will probably peak late in the afternoon and evening, coinciding with school pick-up and the commute home. Many schools have announced early dismissals.

item.title: I’ve seen several types of warfare. This is the worst.
item.description: Drones are erasing any sense of safety, and Iran is only a warmup.

item.title: See which jobs are most threatened by AI and who may be able to adapt
item.description: Women largely hold the most vulnerable occupations. Look up your job to see how at risk it is.

item.title: His ‘favorite Mormon’ in today’s On the Record
item.description: Answer now to earn your Monday points.

item.title: Conan’s jokes, Barbra’s voice and big nights for “One Battle” and Jessie Buckley
item.description: The show was packed with memorable moments as “One Battle After Another” landed six trophies, including best picture, and Buckley completed her award-season sweep.

item.title: Iran war. Media threats. Oscars. Severe weather. Heart health. March Madness. AI job risk. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: ‘One Battle After Another’ wins Oscar for best picture, capping a night in which it took home five other awards
item.description: In addition to the top prize, the film won best director (Paul Thomas Anderson), best adapted screenplay (Anderson, again), best supporting actor (Sean Penn), best casting (Cassandra Kulukundis) and best film editing (Andy Jurgensen).

item.title: Oscars night has arrived. See all the celebrity red carpet looks as the Academy Awards get underway.
item.description: It's a "Sinners" vs. "One Battle After Another" showdown. Butter that popcorn and follow our live coverage.

item.title: Dangerous storms could slam D.C. area Monday, with possibility of tornadoes and torrential rain
item.description: The National Weather Service has declared a rare Level 4 out of 5, or “moderate,” storm risk, meaning severe storms that are expected to be widespread. The storms’ timing still needs to be pinned down, but they are most probable between about 1 and 7 p.m. Monday, when an organized and violent squall line is expected to pass.

item.title: Iran wages economic war in hopes of outlasting U.S. and Israel
item.description: Tehran warned of strikes on banks after hitting data centers, hotels, airports and seaports, threatening the global finance hub of its more prosperous neighbors. The Iranian strategy illustrates the vulnerability of critical links in the global economy.

item.title: DNA testing could clear a dead convict’s name — and point to a serial killer
item.description: Over 34 years in prison, Shawn Tanner maintained he was not guilty of murder. A legal battle over whether the dead can clear their names will grant his final wish.

item.title: Still need to finish this weekend’s On the Record? We’ve got you.
item.description: Play now to finish out your score for the week.

item.title: Presidential appointee wants to replace White House columns with the ones Trump prefers
item.description: The head of a federal arts commission is proposing the more ornate Corinthian style for the nearly 200-year-old columns at the building’s front entrance.

item.title: An ‘epic fail’ by McDonald’s turned into a marketing win
item.description: The video went viral for the wrong reasons. It still won the attention economy, Megan McArdle writes.

item.title: Crossword and a coffee: Solve the Sunday puzzle now
item.description: Play Washington Post's daily crossword puzzles online for free.

item.title: How Iran’s tactics are complicating a quick declaration of victory for Trump
item.description: The U.S. and Israel crippled Iranian forces in the war’s opening stages. Tehran’s uranium stockpile and its ability to disrupt oil flows are frustrating the president's push to end it.

item.title: Richard Grenell’s scorched-earth term at Trump’s Kennedy Center
item.description: The Kennedy Center that Grenell leaves is vastly different from the one he entered one year ago.

item.title: Why stars abandon their significant others for the Oscars red carpet
item.description: With so much at stake in the optics of the red carpet, don’t be surprised if Timothée Chalamet walks without Kylie Jenner.

item.title: Have trouble falling or staying asleep? Try cognitive shuffling.
item.description: Research suggests that this technique may mimic what the brain does naturally when you’re dozing off.

item.title: Six friends started an annual trip. They didn’t realize it would become a lifeline.
item.description: In 1994, six men started the A to Z Club, meeting annually in alphabetical cities. The tradition has evolved from bachelor party antics to deep conversations about life.

item.title: How many guesses will Keyword take you today? Try to beat the average now.
item.description: Keyword, a game from The Washington Post, gives you six words — each missing one letter — and you have to find the word that connects them

item.title: They came to build China’s EV future. Investigators found conditions that recalled ‘slavery.’
item.description: The electric carmaker BYD was hailed as a job creator in a struggling Brazilian city. Then authorities started investigating an improbable tip.
